data.content_ref: inline:{"claim": "No fixed (e, P, R, k) regime is sufficient to predict strict harness advantage; harness strictly beats SC only when the critic is question-adaptive such that its per-item flagging accuracy exceeds a threshold tied to that item's candidate error count, and otherwise the critic's fixed P/R can either help or hurt depending on question-difficulty alignment.", "mechanism": "Items are heterogeneous: each question q has a latent difficulty producing an error-count m_q in {0, ..., k}. A fixed-P/R critic applies the same flagging behavior to every q, but the net effect depends on m_q. For items where m_q >= ceil(k/2) (broken majority), the critic needs high recall on that specific item. For items where m_q < ceil(k/2) (correct majority), the critic needs high precision on that specific item. A critic with fixed aggregate P, R can have high P on easy items and low P on hard items (or vice versa), so the aggregate values do not determine the outcome. Harness strictly beats SC only when per-item critic accuracy is calibrated to the per-item error regime: high R on broken-majority items AND high P on correct-majority items. The observed v4-pro hard data (P=0.125, R=1.0, net=0) is consistent because the 1 item with an error had R=1.0 but the low P did not matter since all other items had m_q=0 (nothing to false-flag that mattered).", "scope": {"covers": ["per-item critic calibration as the key variable", "fixed-P/R critics acting on heterogeneous question pools", "decomposing net gain into per-item contributions conditional on m_q"], "excludes": ["any prediction from aggregate P, R alone", "homogeneous-question pools where all items share the same m_q", "regimes where the critic has access to the answer key"]}, "forbidden": [{"case": "Two experiments with identical aggregate (e=0.30, P=0.7, R=0.8, k=5) but different per-item critic calibration produce the same harness-vs-SC gap (contradicting the claim that calibration, not aggregate values, determines the outcome)", "eval": "rubric:std-crit-design"}, {"case": "A critic with perfect per-item calibration (R=1 on broken-majority items, P=1 on correct-majority items) fails to strictly beat SC at e=0.30, k=5 (contradicting the sufficiency claim)", "eval": "rubric:std-crit-design"}, {"case": "A critic with aggregate P=0.8, R=0.8 at e=0.30, k=5 strictly beats SC on one question set but strictly loses to SC on another with the same aggregate statistics (directly confirming per-item heterogeneity dominates)", "eval": "rubric:std-crit-design"}], "prose_notes": "This supersedes the fixed-regime candidate by arguing that the observed null results are not explained by aggregate P/R alone but by the alignment between per-item critic behavior and per-item error structure. The decisive experiment must hold aggregate P/R approximately fixed while varying per-item calibration — achievable by sorting questions by difficulty and using two critic variants (one difficulty-correlated, one anti-correlated). Budget: 60 items * 2 critic variants * (5 candidates + 5 critiques + 5 re-votes) * ~500 tokens = ~300k tokens, at the budget boundary. If budget is tight, reduce to 40 items."}
